senses.definition: plural of baton: a thin stick used by a conductor, drum major, or relay runner.
examples: The conductor raised the batons and began the performance.
The relay team dropped the batons during the race.

senses.definition: plural of baton: a short, heavy stick used as a weapon or symbol of authority.
examples: The police officers carried batons on their belts.
The guard struck the shield with his batons.

senses.definition: an old word for a baton or staff.
examples: In older texts, a batoon could mean a staff or baton.
The heraldic figure was described as a batoon.

senses.definition: decorated or marked with a batonlike shape or band, especially in heraldry; also used in older technical senses.
examples: The shield was batooned in the old heraldic description.
The manuscript used a batooned form of the emblem.

senses.definition: the action of using or handling a baton; in older or specialist use, an act of batonlike marking or shaping.
examples: The report mentioned the batooning of the ceremonial staff.
Batooning was described in the old manual.

senses.definition: present participle of batoon, mainly in older or very rare use.
examples: The text described soldiers batooning in a ceremonial drill.
He was batooning the staff in the account.

senses.definition: plural of batoon: old word for batons or staffs.
examples: The museum displayed several batoons from the period.
Heraldic batoons appeared in the old drawings.

senses.definition: an abnormal fear of heights or of being near very tall objects.
examples: She felt sick when she stood near the glass tower because of her batophobia.
His batophobia made mountain walks very difficult.

senses.definition: a taxonomic group of amphibians, especially frogs and salamanders; also used as a collective term for frogs.
examples: The lecture discussed the evolution of batrachia.
Batrachia includes frogs and salamanders in some classifications.

senses.definition: an amphibian, especially a frog or salamander.
examples: The scientist studied a batrachian from the rainforest.
Many batrachians live in wet habitats.

senses.definition: relating to frogs, salamanders, or amphibians.
examples: The paper described batrachian anatomy.
Batrachian traits are important in the fossil record.

senses.definition: plural of batrachian: amphibians, especially frogs and salamanders.
examples: The pond was full of batrachians in spring.
The museum had jars containing batrachians.

senses.definition: having the form or characteristics of a frog or amphibian.
examples: The fossil showed batrachiate features.
Some researchers described the creature as batrachiate.

senses.definition: a former or technical taxonomic name for a group of frogs; used in zoological classification.
examples: The book listed Batrachidae among older family names.
Batrachidae appears in historical taxonomy.

senses.definition: a fossil or mineral term used in older scientific writing, often referring to a stone or fossil associated with amphibian remains.
examples: The collector identified a batrachite in the cabinet.
Batrachite is mentioned in older natural-history texts.

senses.definition: a scientific or anatomical term related to a frog or amphibian, used in specialist Latin-based terminology.
examples: The text used batrachium in a taxonomic discussion.
Batrachium appears in older zoological labels.

senses.definition: a fish of the batrachoid family; a toadfish.
examples: The marine biologist identified the batrachoid near the reef.
Batrachoids are bottom-dwelling fish.

senses.definition: relating to toadfish or the batrachoid family.
examples: The study focused on batrachoid characteristics.
Batrachoid anatomy was described in detail.

senses.definition: the family of toadfishes.
examples: Batrachoididae includes several species of toadfish.
The guidebook listed Batrachoididae under marine fishes.

senses.definition: feeding on frogs.
examples: The animal was described as batrachophagous in the article.
Some snakes are batrachophagous predators.

senses.definition: a scientific or technical term connected with frogs or amphibians, used in older zoological writing.
examples: The paper mentioned batrachophidia in a historical note.
Batrachophidia is rare in modern English.

senses.definition: an abnormal fear of frogs or amphibians.
examples: Her batrachophobia made garden ponds stressful places.
He avoided the lake because of his batrachophobia.

senses.definition: plural of batrachophobia: abnormal fears of frogs or amphibians.
examples: The clinic treated several batrachophobias.
Different batrachophobias can vary in intensity.

senses.definition: having an abnormal fear of frogs or amphibians.
examples: He became batrachophobic after an unpleasant childhood experience.
The patient reported batrachophobic reactions near ponds.

senses.definition: a surgical repair or reconstruction involving the frog-like part of a structure; a rare technical term.
examples: Batrachoplasty appears in specialist medical terminology.
The handbook listed batrachoplasty as a rare procedure.

senses.definition: a genus of freshwater red algae.
examples: Batrachospermum grows in clean freshwater streams.
The sample was identified as Batrachospermum.

senses.definition: a highly poisonous substance found in some poison dart frogs and related animals.
examples: Batrachotoxin is one of the most toxic natural poisons known.
The frog's skin contained batrachotoxin.

senses.definition: plural of bat: small flying mammals that come out at night.
examples: Bats flew out of the cave at dusk.
Some bats use echolocation to find food.

senses.definition: plural of bat: wooden or metal sports equipment used to hit a ball.
examples: The players picked up their bats and walked onto the field.
He broke two bats during the match.

senses.definition: third person singular present of bat: to hit something with a bat, or to move the eyelids quickly.
examples: She bats the ball well in practice.
He bats his eyes when he is joking.

senses.definition: a cricket player who is batting or whose main role is to score runs.
examples: The batsman defended the wicket carefully.
A left-handed batsman opened the innings.
